**Zhenjiang**, [alternately](/wiki/Postal_map_romanization "Postal map romanization") [romanized](/wiki/Romanization_of_Chinese "Romanization of Chinese") as **Chinkiang**, is a [prefecture-level city](/wiki/Prefecture-level_city "Prefecture-level city") in [Jiangsu](/wiki/Jiangsu "Jiangsu") Province, China. It lies on the southern [bank](/wiki/Bank_(geography) "Bank (geography)") of the [Yangtze River](/wiki/Yangtze_River "Yangtze River") near its intersection with the [Grand Canal](/wiki/Grand_Canal_of_China "Grand Canal of China"). It is opposite [Yangzhou](/wiki/Yangzhou "Yangzhou") (to its north) and between [Nanjing](/wiki/Nanjing "Nanjing") (to its west) and [Changzhou](/wiki/Changzhou "Changzhou") (to its east). Zhenjiang was formerly the provincial capital of Jiangsu and remains as an important transportation hub. As of the [2020 census](/wiki/2020_Chinese_census "2020 Chinese census"), its total population was 3,210,418 inhabitants whom 1,266,790 lived in the built-up (or metro) area made of the 3 urban districts. The town is best known both in China and abroad for [Chinkiang vinegar](/wiki/Chinkiang_vinegar "Chinkiang vinegar"), a fragrant [black vinegar](/wiki/Black_vinegar "Black vinegar") that is a staple of [Chinese cooking](/wiki/Chinese_cooking "Chinese cooking").

## Names

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Zhenjiang&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Names")]

Prior to the adoption of [Hanyu Pinyin](/wiki/Hanyu_Pinyin "Hanyu Pinyin"), the city's name was typically [romanized](/wiki/Romanization_of_Chinese "Romanization of Chinese") as **Chin-keang-foo**,[[3]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTE''EB''1878-3) **Chen-kiang-fu**,[[4]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTE''EB''1911-4) or **Chinkiang**.[[4]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTE''EB''1911-4)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Zhenjiang&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

### Early history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Zhenjiang&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Early history")]

Human activity in Zhenjiang dates to the early Neolithic period. Excavations at the Zuohu (左湖) site reveal a continuous cultural sequence from the [Majiabang](/wiki/Majiabang_culture "Majiabang culture") to the Bronze Age Hushu culture. Adapted to the Ning-Zhen Hills, prehistoric inhabitants developed distinct ground-level dwellings that differ from contemporary Majiabang sites in the plains. The area is considered one of the early centers of rice cultivation in the lower Yangtze River valley.[[5]](#cite_note-LinWang2000-5)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6e/20251026_%22Yi_Hou_Ce%22_Bronze_Gui.jpg/250px-20251026_%22Yi_Hou_Ce%22_Bronze_Gui.jpg)](/wiki/File:20251026_%22Yi_Hou_Ce%22_Bronze_Gui.jpg)

The Yi Hou Ze-*[gui](/wiki/Gui_(vessel) "Gui (vessel)")* (宜侯夨簋), National Museum of China

The Yi Hou Ze-*[gui](/wiki/Gui_(vessel) "Gui (vessel)")* (宜侯夨簋) unearthed at Dantu records the [Zhou](/wiki/Zhou_dynasty "Zhou dynasty") investiture of an aristocrat at "**Yi**," presumed to be the region where the vessel was discovered, including the granting of land and people, namely *[fengjian](/wiki/Fengjian "Fengjian")*.[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7) Despite ongoing scholarly debate, it remains the only bronze inscription interpreted as providing epigraphical support for [Sima Qian](/wiki/Sima_Qian "Sima Qian")'s account of Wu [Taibo](/wiki/Taibo "Taibo")'s enfeoffment in the Jiangnan region.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

During the Spring and Autumn period, the region was incorporated into the [State of Wu](/wiki/Wu_(state) "Wu (state)") and first appeared in historical records under the name **Zhufang** (朱方). In 543 BC, Qing Feng, a minister from the [State of Qi](/wiki/Qi_(state) "Qi (state)"), fled to Wu seeking asylum following a failed power struggle. King Yuji of Wu granted him Zhufang as a fief. However, in 537 BC, King Ling of Chu led a coalition of feudal lords to besiege and capture Zhufang, subsequently executing Feng.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

Until the 3rd century CE, the apex of the Yangtze River's funnel-shaped estuary was located near present-day Zhenjiang and Yangzhou. During the early imperial era, the counties of **Dantu** (丹徒) and **Qu'e** (曲阿) were established on the narrow coastal plains of this region. They served as major transit hubs for overland and river transport, linking the imperial center with the Lake Tai and Qiantang River regions.

At the turn of the 3rd century CE, the Sun clan—who later founded the state of Eastern Wu—used Dantu as a primary administrative base, and the family patriarch Sun Jian was interred in nearby Qu'e. In 209, Sun Quan renamed this base "Jing" (京), which subsequently became known as **Jingkou** (京口).[[10]](#cite_note-:1-10) Situated about 80 km east of Nanjing (then Jianye or Jiankang), Jingkou developed into a strategic gateway on the lower Yangtze and an important military outpost guarding the capital.

In 211, Sun Quan moved his seat of government to Jianye. To improve transport links between the new capital and the Kuaiji region while bypassing the navigational hazards of the Yangtze estuary, the Eastern Wu regime commissioned the Pogang Canal (破岡瀆) and other waterways through the Ning–Zhen Hills. These canals connected the capital with its surrounding agricultural heartlands.

Although located at a strategic transport hub, early Jingkou remained sparsely populated and underdeveloped. A hunting preserve southeast of the town served the Sun clan, while much of the surrounding land was organized as state farms (tuntian) under an agricultural command based in Biling (毗陵).

### Medieval Period

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Zhenjiang&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Medieval Period")]

Following the fall of the Central Plains and the southward retreat of the imperial court, hundreds of thousands of displaced northerners—many of them from north of the Huai River—were resettled in and around Jingkou and the neighboring Zhenjiang and Changzhou areas.[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETwitchettFairbank1978332-3-11)

In the early 4th century, Xi Jian—a general of northern émigré origin and regional governor of Xu—strategically garrisoned Jingkou to counter internal rebellions. He relocated his military headquarters and the nominal Xu provincial administration from the north bank of the Yangtze to Jingkou, establishing the new "Northern Headquarters" (*Beifu*, 北府) there.[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETian2012287-92-12)

Jingkou thus developed into a major garrison for controlling Jiangnan, defending the capital Nanjing, and resisting seaborne incursions.[[13]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETian201270-95-13) Later, [Xie Xuan](/wiki/Xie_Xuan "Xie Xuan") was appointed to command the lower Yangtze defenses. Building on the demographic and military base laid by Xi Jian, he recruited and trained elite troops that became known as the Northern Headquarters Troops (*beifu bing*).[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETwitchettFairbank1978112-3-14)

In the late 4th century, [Wang Gong](/wiki/Wang_Gong_(Jin_dynasty) "Wang Gong (Jin dynasty)"), then in command at Jingkou, sought to enhance his position by appointing the *Beifu Bing* general [Liu Laozhi](/wiki/Liu_Laozhi "Liu Laozhi") as his *Sima* (chief of staff). The court figure [Sima Yuanxian](/wiki/Sima_Yuanxian "Sima Yuanxian"), however, induced Liu to defect by promising him Wang's office. After Wang was killed, military command and the regional governorship passed to Liu Laozhi, increasing the autonomy of the *Beifubing* and marking a shift in military leadership from the great aristocratic clans toward lower-ranking elites.

With the rise of [Emperor Wu of Song](/wiki/Emperor_Wu_of_Song "Emperor Wu of Song") Liu Yu, commander of the *Beifu Bing* and a descendant of the immigrant communities at Jingkou, the *Beifubing* were turned into a royal army. In his final instructions, Liu Yu stipulated that Jingkou should be held only by close members of the imperial clan. After the Liu Song dynasty recovered territories in the north, the administration at Jingkou was renamed South Xuzhou (Nan Xuzhou) to distinguish it from the original Xuzhou governed from [Pengcheng](/wiki/Xuzhou "Xuzhou").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a3/Segalen-25-Qi-Wudi-Chimera-left.jpg/250px-Segalen-25-Qi-Wudi-Chimera-left.jpg)](/wiki/File:Segalen-25-Qi-Wudi-Chimera-left.jpg)

"Chimeras" at the tomb of Emperor Wu of Southern Qi (Xiao Ze) in Danyang

Owing to their status under the émigré administrative system, the northern settlers at Jingkou initially enjoyed exemptions from taxes and corvée labor.[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETwitchettFairbank1978334-5-15) In 454, during the reign of [Emperor Xiaowu of Song](/wiki/Emperor_Xiaowu_of_Song "Emperor Xiaowu of Song"), the court began to levy taxes on these populations, and the institutional status of the Northern Headquarters gradually declined, and was abolished in 484. In 502, the Liang government formally ended the remaining exemptions for immigrant communities.

The [Sui](/wiki/Sui_dynasty "Sui dynasty") took the city in AD 581 from [Chen](/wiki/Chen_dynasty "Chen dynasty") and made it an important garrison on the lower [Yangtze](/wiki/Yangtze "Yangtze"), the source of its present name ("Protecting the River"). In 595, it was restored to commandery status. Its importance grew with the construction of the [Grand Canal](/wiki/Grand_Canal_of_China "Grand Canal of China"), after which it served as the chief collection and transit center for the grain tax paid by the farmers of the [Yangtze delta](/wiki/Yangtze_delta "Yangtze delta").

### Song to Qing dynasties

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Zhenjiang&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Song to Qing dynasties")]

The city flourished from the 10th to 13th centuries, when it produced fine silks, satins, and silverware for the [Song emperors](/wiki/List_of_Song_emperors "List of Song emperors"). The 11th-century scientist and statesman [Shen Kuo](/wiki/Shen_Kuo "Shen Kuo") composed his 1088 *[Dream Pool Essays](/wiki/Dream_Pool_Essays "Dream Pool Essays")* during his retirement in a garden estate on the outskirts of the city. It was taken by the [Mongolians](/wiki/Mongolian_Empire "Mongolian Empire") during their 1275 campaign against the [Southern Song](/wiki/Southern_Song "Southern Song") capital at [Hangzhou](/wiki/Hangzhou "Hangzhou").[[16]](#cite_note-:2-16) Under their [Yuan dynasty](/wiki/Yuan_dynasty "Yuan dynasty"), some [Nestorian](/wiki/Nestorian_Christianity "Nestorian Christianity") [Christians](/wiki/Christianity_in_China "Christianity in China") were reported living in the city. The city fell to [Xu Da](/wiki/Xu_Da "Xu Da") on 17 March 1356.[[17]](#cite_note-17) According to [Odoric of Pordenone](/wiki/Odoric_of_Pordenone "Odoric of Pordenone"), Zhenjiang had a vast amount of shipping, more so than any other city in the world. The ships which worked the city were painted white and often doubled as businesses such as taverns or other gathering spots.[[18]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYule2002133-18) Under the [Ming](/wiki/Ming_dynasty "Ming dynasty"), it was the seat of a prefecture ([fu](/wiki/Fu_(administrative_division) "Fu (administrative division)")) of [Nanzhili](/wiki/Nanzhili "Nanzhili"), the Southern Directly-Administered District around the secondary capital [Nanjing](/wiki/Nanjing "Nanjing"). The [Southern Ming](/wiki/Southern_Ming "Southern Ming") placed the town under [Zheng Zhifeng](/w/index.php?title=Zheng_Zhifeng&action=edit&redlink=1 "Zheng Zhifeng (page does not exist)"), brother of [Zheng Zhilong](/wiki/Zheng_Zhilong "Zheng Zhilong") and favorite uncle of [Koxinga](/wiki/Koxinga "Koxinga"). He was fooled into wasting most of his ammunition against a feint, however, and forced to abandon the city to the [Manchus](/wiki/Manchu_people "Manchu people") on 1 June 1645.[[19]](#cite_note-:3-19)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b7/Chinkiang.jpg/250px-Chinkiang.jpg)](/wiki/File:Chinkiang.jpg)

Zhenjiang Prefecture ("Chinkiang") between the [Yangtze](/wiki/Yangtze "Yangtze") and [Lake Tai](/wiki/Lake_Tai "Lake Tai") east of [Nanjing](/wiki/Nanjing "Nanjing") ("Kiangning"), from [Martino Martini](/wiki/Martino_Martini "Martino Martini")'s 1655 *[Novus Atlas Sinensis](/wiki/Novus_Atlas_Sinensis "Novus Atlas Sinensis")*. The river marked west of the city is the [Grand Canal](/wiki/Grand_Canal_of_China "Grand Canal of China").

Under the [Qing](/wiki/Qing_dynasty "Qing dynasty"), Zhenjiang was a city of half a million surrounded by a series of brick [city walls](/wiki/Chinese_city_walls "Chinese city walls") up to 35 feet (11 m) high.[[3]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTE''EB''1878-3) It continued as a prefectural seat, first under the "Right" Governor of [Jiangnan](/wiki/Jiangnan_Province "Jiangnan Province") at [Suzhou](/wiki/Suzhou,_Jiangsu "Suzhou, Jiangsu") and later under the governor of [Jiangsu](/wiki/Jiangsu "Jiangsu") in Jiangning (now Nanjing). After a fierce resistance, Zhenjiang—romanized at the time as Chinkiang—was [captured](/wiki/Battle_of_Chinkiang "Battle of Chinkiang") by the [British](/wiki/United_Kingdom_of_Great_Britain_and_Ireland "United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland") on 21 July 1842 during the [First Opium War](/wiki/First_Opium_War "First Opium War").[[3]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTE''EB''1878-3)[[4]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTE''EB''1911-4) As this left the path open to [Nanjing](/wiki/Nanjing "Nanjing"), its fall prompted the [unequal](/wiki/Unequal_Treaties "Unequal Treaties") [Treaty of Nanking](/wiki/Treaty_of_Nanking "Treaty of Nanking") to avoid further conflict. A decade later, massive floods of the [Yellow River](/wiki/Yellow_River "Yellow River") altered its course from south to north of [Shandong](/wiki/Shandong "Shandong") and closed the northern path of the Grand Canal. Soon after, Zhenjiang was sacked by the [Taiping rebels](/wiki/Taiping_Rebellion "Taiping Rebellion") in 1853.[[3]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTE''EB''1878-3) It was recaptured by the Qing in 1858[[3]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTE''EB''1878-3) and opened as a [treaty port](/wiki/Treaty_port "Treaty port") in 1861.[[20]](#cite_note-:4-20) Into the 1870s, [Chaozhou](/wiki/Chaozhou "Chaozhou") merchants used their connections in Zhenjiang to make it a regional distribution center for [opium](/wiki/History_of_opium_in_China "History of opium in China") purchased from the foreign merchants in [Shanghai](/wiki/Shanghai "Shanghai"); when [David Sassoon](/wiki/David_Sassoon_(treasurer) "David Sassoon (treasurer)") attempted to avoid taxation by delivering his cargoes directly to the opium merchants in Zhenjiang, the Chinese organized to intimidate his customers and then bought out his failed organization.[[21]](#cite_note-21) The population was estimated at 168,000 in 1904.[[4]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTE''EB''1911-4)

### Modern China

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Zhenjiang&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Modern China")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/26/Former_Consulate_of_UK_in_Zhenjiang_2011-10.JPG/250px-Former_Consulate_of_UK_in_Zhenjiang_2011-10.JPG)](/wiki/File:Former_Consulate_of_UK_in_Zhenjiang_2011-10.JPG)

The former British consulate in Zhenjiang in 2011

The southern part of the Grand Canal was obstructed in the early 20th century,[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] although by that point the city was connected by [rail](/wiki/History_of_rail_in_China "History of rail in China") to [Shanghai](/wiki/Shanghai "Shanghai") and [Nanjing](/wiki/Nanjing "Nanjing").[[4]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTE''EB''1911-4) The [Kuomintang](/wiki/Nationalist "Nationalist") government revoked the British concession at Zhenjiang in 1929.

From 1928 to 1949, while [Nanjing](/wiki/Nanjing "Nanjing") served as the capital of the [Republic of China](/wiki/Republic_of_China_(1912%E2%80%931949) "Republic of China (1912–1949)"), Zhenjiang served as the provincial capital for Jiangsu. During [World War II](/wiki/Second_Sino-Japanese_War "Second Sino-Japanese War"), the city fell to [Japan](/wiki/Empire_of_Japan "Empire of Japan")'s [Shanghai Expeditionary Army](/wiki/Shanghai_Expeditionary_Army "Shanghai Expeditionary Army") in the morning of 8 December 1937,[[22]](#cite_note-neno-22) shortly before [the capture of Nanjing](/wiki/Battle_of_Nanking "Battle of Nanking"), but local resistance to the Japanese is still celebrated among the Chinese.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] When the [Communists](/wiki/Communist_Party_of_China "Communist Party of China") won the [Chinese Civil War](/wiki/Chinese_Civil_War "Chinese Civil War") and relocated the capital to [Beijing](/wiki/Beijing "Beijing"), Nanjing resumed its role as Jiangsu's capital.

Zhenjiang is still one of China's busiest ports for domestic commerce, serving as a hub for trade among [Jiangsu](/wiki/Jiangsu "Jiangsu"), [Anhui](/wiki/Anhui "Anhui"), and [Shanghai](/wiki/Shanghai "Shanghai"). The trade mostly consists of grain, cotton, oils, and lumber. The other main industries are mostly in the field of food processing and paper pulp manufacturing.

### Climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Zhenjiang&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Climate")]

The city has a [humid subtropical climate](/wiki/Humid_subtropical_climate "Humid subtropical climate") (Koppen:Cwa), with a noticeable rise in rainfall during the [East Asian monsoon](/wiki/East_Asian_monsoon "East Asian monsoon"). Extremes since 1951 have ranged from −12.0 °C (10 °F) (unofficial record of −12.9 °C (9 °F) was set on 27 January 1933)[[23]](#cite_note-23) to 39.5 °C (103 °F).

## Demographics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Zhenjiang&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Demographics")]

As in [Nanjing](/wiki/Nanjing_dialect "Nanjing dialect"), Zhenjiang's old [Wu dialects](/wiki/Wu_Chinese "Wu Chinese") have been entirely supplanted by [a dialect](/wiki/Zhenjiang_dialect "Zhenjiang dialect") of [Lower Yangtze Mandarin](/wiki/Lower_Yangtze_Mandarin "Lower Yangtze Mandarin").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] It is incomprehensible to the residents of neighboring [Changzhou](/wiki/Changzhou "Changzhou"), [whose dialect](/wiki/Changzhou_dialect "Changzhou dialect") remains a form of [Taihu Wu](/wiki/Taihu_Wu "Taihu Wu").

The population was 3,210,418 as of 2020, reflecting a 0.31% annual change from the 2010 census, which recorded a population of 3,114,105.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Zhenjiang&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Culture")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/66/Jinshan_temple.JPG/250px-Jinshan_temple.JPG)](/wiki/File:Jinshan_temple.JPG)

The Jinshan Temple

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f2/Longchangsi13.jpg/250px-Longchangsi13.jpg)](/wiki/File:Longchangsi13.jpg)

The roof of Longchang Temple

Zhenjiang is most famous for its fragrant black vinegar, called [Zhenjiang vinegar](/wiki/Zhenjiang_vinegar "Zhenjiang vinegar"). Chinese legend traces it to Heita, the son of [Dukang](/wiki/Dukang "Dukang"), the supposed inventor of [alcoholic beverages](/wiki/Chinese_alcoholic_beverages "Chinese alcoholic beverages"). Having forgotten about a vat of wine for 21 days, he found it had spoiled but now possessed a pleasant sour taste that could be used to complement foods.[[28]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChen_&_al.2009[httpsbooksgooglecombooksidXuPWgEMx_eICpgPA243_243]-28) The present recipe is said to date back 1400 years, with its major modern manufacturer—the Jiangsu Hengshun Vinegar Industry Co.—dating to 1840.[[29]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChen_&_al.2009[httpsbooksgooglecombooksidXuPWgEMx_eICpgPA254_254]-29)

Other local specialties include crab cream bun,[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] Chinkiang pork (鎭江肴肉, akin to [head cheese](/wiki/Head_cheese "Head cheese")), and pickled vegetables.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Formerly, households in Zhenjiang would prepare for the [new year](/wiki/Chinese_New_Year "Chinese New Year") by eating a [red-bean](/wiki/Adzuki_bean "Adzuki bean") dish and avoiding rice. One bowl of beans was left on the table to feed the home's [flies](/wiki/Housefly "Housefly"), from the belief that they would then avoid disturbing the family during the new year festivities.[[30]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELee2005[httpsbooksgooglecombooksid4cRgDQAAQBAJpgPA236_236]-30)

A [natural spring](/wiki/Natural_spring "Natural spring") in a park on the edge of Zhenjiang has been famed since the [Tang](/wiki/Tang_dynasty "Tang dynasty") (7th–9th century) as the best in Jiangsu for making [tea](/wiki/Chinese_tea "Chinese tea"). It is now marketed as the "First Spring [under Heaven](/wiki/Tianxia "Tianxia")".

The 15th-century [Japanese](/wiki/Japanese_painting "Japanese painting") [ink-wash](/wiki/Ink_wash_painting "Ink wash painting") master [Sesshū Tōyō](/wiki/Sessh%C5%AB_T%C5%8Dy%C5%8D "Sesshū Tōyō") studied in Zhenjiang.[[22]](#cite_note-neno-22)

The local Jinshan temple appears in the tale of *[Madame White Snake](/wiki/Madame_White_Snake "Madame White Snake")* and inspired a replica in the [Kangxi Emperor](/wiki/Kangxi_Emperor "Kangxi Emperor")'s garden at [Chengde](/wiki/Chengde_Mountain_Resort "Chengde Mountain Resort").[[31]](#cite_note-31)

## Transport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Zhenjiang&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Transport")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/82/Zhenjiang_CRH_Railway_station.jpg/250px-Zhenjiang_CRH_Railway_station.jpg)](/wiki/File:Zhenjiang_CRH_Railway_station.jpg)

The new Zhenjiang Railway Station

Zhenjiang is located in the convenient [Yangtze River Delta](/wiki/Yangtze_River_Delta "Yangtze River Delta") transport corridor, at the crossroads of the [Grand Canal](/wiki/Grand_Canal_(China) "Grand Canal (China)") and the [Yangtze](/wiki/Yangtze_River "Yangtze River"), and between the Shanghai and Nanjing economic regions. The [Port of Zhenjiang](/wiki/Port_of_Zhenjiang "Port of Zhenjiang") is the third largest port on the Yangtze. The city has two [Yangtze River crossings](/wiki/Yangtze_River_bridges_and_tunnels "Yangtze River bridges and tunnels"). The [Runyang Yangtze River Bridge complex](/wiki/Runyang_Yangtze_River_Bridge "Runyang Yangtze River Bridge"), which has one of the [longest suspension bridge spans](/wiki/List_of_longest_suspension_bridge_spans "List of longest suspension bridge spans") in the world, connects to [Yangzhou](/wiki/Yangzhou "Yangzhou"). The [Taizhou Yangtze River Bridge](/wiki/Taizhou_Yangtze_River_Bridge "Taizhou Yangtze River Bridge"), [one of the longest cable-stayed bridges in the world](/wiki/List_of_longest_cable-stayed_bridge_spans "List of longest cable-stayed bridge spans"), connects Yongzhong with Taizhou.

### Rail

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Zhenjiang&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Rail")]

Zhenjiang has been connected by rail since 1906, at the completion of the Nanjing-Shanghai Railway. The railway was extended to Beijing after the completion of the [Nanjing Yangtze River Bridge](/wiki/Nanjing_Yangtze_River_Bridge "Nanjing Yangtze River Bridge") in 1968, connecting Zhenjiang to China's political and commercial hubs. The primary railway station was Zhenjiang West Railway Station, which was demolished in 2004 due to congestion it caused in the city center. Since then [Zhenjiang Railway Station](/wiki/Zhenjiang_Railway_Station "Zhenjiang Railway Station") has served as the city's principal railway station.

#### High-speed rail

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Zhenjiang&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: High-speed rail")]

Since April 2010, Zhenjiang has been on the route of the [Shanghai-Nanjing Intercity Rail](/wiki/Shanghai-Nanjing_Intercity_Rail "Shanghai-Nanjing Intercity Rail"), the first high-speed rail with a design speed of over 300 km/h (186 mph) to serve the city. In 2011, the [Beijing–Shanghai High-Speed Railway](/wiki/Beijing%E2%80%93Shanghai_High-Speed_Railway "Beijing–Shanghai High-Speed Railway") was completed. Trains on the line stop at [Zhenjiang South Railway Station](/wiki/Zhenjiang_South_Railway_Station "Zhenjiang South Railway Station"). The two high-speed lines have reduced travel time between Zhenjiang and Shanghai to under an hour, and travel time to Beijing to under five hours. Rail service to Shanghai is frequent - averaging one train in less than half an hour.[[32]](#cite_note-32)

### Air

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Zhenjiang&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Air")]

Zhenjiang does not have a commercial airport within its city limits, although there is a military airfield, [Zhenjiang Dalu Airport](/w/index.php?title=Zhenjiang_Dalu_Airport&action=edit&redlink=1 "Zhenjiang Dalu Airport (page does not exist)") (镇江大路机场), which may open to regional flights in the future. Zhenjiang city center is 62 km (39 mi) away from [Changzhou Benniu International Airport](/wiki/Changzhou_Benniu_International_Airport "Changzhou Benniu International Airport"), about a one-hour drive (80 km or 50 mi) away from [Nanjing Lukou International Airport](/wiki/Nanjing_Lukou_International_Airport "Nanjing Lukou International Airport") via Nanjing Provincial Highway 243, and approximately a two-hour (143 km or 89 mi) drive away from [Sunan Shuofang International Airport](/wiki/Sunan_Shuofang_International_Airport "Sunan Shuofang International Airport"). Check-in facilities are available for Lukou Airport in the New Zhenjiang Bus Station (镇江汽车新站).[[33]](#cite_note-renmin-33)

### Roadways

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Zhenjiang&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Roadways")]

Zhenjiang is on the route of [Beijing-Shanghai Expressway](/wiki/Beijing-Shanghai_Expressway "Beijing-Shanghai Expressway"), and [China National Highway 312](/wiki/China_National_Highway_312 "China National Highway 312").

### Public transport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Zhenjiang&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Public transport")]

As of 2014[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Zhenjiang&action=edit), Zhenjiang had an extensive number of bus routes - numbering nearly one hundred. Since 2012 the city's entire fleet of city buses are equipped with GPS and are managed centrally through a "smart transport network system."[[33]](#cite_note-renmin-33)

## Industry

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Zhenjiang&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Industry")]

Zhenjiang Export Processing Zone was approved by the [State Council](/wiki/State_Council_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China "State Council of the People's Republic of China") on March 10, 2003, with a total planned area of 2.53 square kilometres (0.98 square miles). The first-phrase project completed in December 2003 covers 0.91 square kilometres (0.35 square miles) and was certified by the Customs General Administration and other seven ministries for operation on December 24, 2003. Zhenjiang Export Processing Zone is located close to [Changzhou Airport](/wiki/Changzhou_Benniu_Airport "Changzhou Benniu Airport") and Zhenjiang Port.[[34]](#cite_note-34)

## Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Zhenjiang&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Education")]

As of 2025, Zhenjiang is a major city for scientific research, appearing among the world's top 100 [cities](/wiki/List_of_cities_by_scientific_output "List of cities by scientific output") as tracked by the [Nature Index](/wiki/Nature_Index "Nature Index").[[35]](#cite_note-35)

Public institutions having full-time [Bachelor's degree](/wiki/Bachelor%27s_degree "Bachelor's degree") programs include [Jiangsu University](/wiki/Jiangsu_University "Jiangsu University") (江苏大学) and the [Jiangsu University of Science and Technology](/w/index.php?title=Jiangsu_University_of_Science_and_Technology&action=edit&redlink=1 "Jiangsu University of Science and Technology (page does not exist)") (江苏科技大学). Zhenjiang is home to the Silkworm Raising Research Institute of the [Academy of Agricultural Science of China](/wiki/Chinese_Academy_of_Agricultural_Sciences "Chinese Academy of Agricultural Sciences"). The Shaozong Library includes a 100-volume collection of sayings and proverbs dating from the 7th to 11th centuries.

Senior high schools are Jiangsu Provincial Zhenjiang No. 1 High School (江苏省镇江第一中学), the Zhenjiang High School of Jiangsu Province (江苏省镇江中学) and the Jiangsu Provincial Dagang High School (江苏省大港中学).
